findings.title: AskUserQuestion only renders the first question and the reply path hardcodes `q_0`
findings.description: The new UI path calls `extract_first_question()` and ignores every entry after `questions[0]`, so a valid multi-question prompt will only show one question to the user. The downstream response builder added in `src/subprocess/parser.rs` also always writes `answers: {"q_0": ...}`. If the upstream tool sends 2-3 questions, the bot will return an incomplete `updatedInput` payload and the interaction flow becomes incorrect even though the user completed the prompt.
findings.suggestion: Either reject multi-question inputs up front, or render all questions and return a complete `answers` map keyed by each question id/index instead of always using only the first question and `q_0`.

findings.title: Question select menus do not enforce Discord's 25-option limit
findings.description: `create_question_message()` switches to a string select menu for `options.len() >= 6`, but it never caps or validates the option count. Discord rejects select menus with more than 25 options, so a larger AskUserQuestion payload will make `send_message` fail and the permission handler will fall back to denying the request. This is a runtime failure path, not just a cosmetic issue.
findings.suggestion: Validate the incoming option count before building components. Reject oversized inputs explicitly, or truncate/split them so no select menu exceeds 25 options.

findings.title: Modal submit path skips the same authorization check used for buttons and selects
findings.description: The button and select handlers re-read `pending_permissions[request_id]` and require `triggered_by` or owner access before consuming the pending request. `handle_modal_interaction()` parses the modal `custom_id`, acknowledges it, removes the pending entry, and sends `PermissionDecision::Answer(...)` without verifying the submitting user. That makes authorization inconsistent across interaction types and allows any accepted modal submit with the same `request_id` to consume the pending request.
findings.suggestion: Mirror the button/select authorization logic in `handle_modal_interaction()` before acknowledging or removing the pending request.

findings.title: New question interaction `custom_id`s are built from an unbounded `request_id`
findings.description: `ask:{request_id}:{index}`, `ask_text:{request_id}`, `ask_sel:{request_id}`, and `ask_modal:{request_id}` all interpolate the raw request id with no length guard. Discord limits `custom_id` to 100 characters, so longer request ids will start failing only at runtime. The modal path is especially tight because the prefix itself is already 10 characters.
findings.suggestion: Bound or hash the request id before embedding it in component/modal `custom_id`s, and add tests that assert every generated id stays within 100 characters.

summary: The diff is close on basic async flow, but it has three functional issues: multi-question prompts are not handled correctly, select menus can exceed Discord limits, and modal submissions do not enforce the same authorization checks as the other interaction paths.
